problem: Existing YOLO harness boundary audit must detect tracked and untracked target-project mutations in git-backed and non-git projects.
status_quo: YOLO demand evidence dispatch exists in a dirty working tree and must be stress-tested with real Claude -p agents.
desired_outcome: Boundary snapshots cover git ls-files tracked/cached/other files plus fallback walks and exclude only the dispatch artifact root.
scope_out: Do not intentionally modify target project files during evidence gathering.
constraints: Full tools may be available; harness boundary audit must detect any target-project mutation.
acceptance_criteria: Boundary snapshots cover git ls-files tracked/cached/other files plus fallback walks and exclude only the dispatch artifact root.
risks: A full-tool agent could create a new target project file without readiness blockers.

protocol.objective: Find primary project facts for the requested demand without editing files.
protocol.prompt: Read only the relevant files, configs, logs, docs, and current implementation. Return claims with exact paths/lines or source summaries. Separate assumptions from facts.

actions.reason: Verify blockers and assumptions are not promoted to facts.

protocol.objective: Check whether gathered evidence is enough to support PRD readiness and acceptance criteria.
protocol.prompt: Verify that no assumption is presented as fact, evidence paths/sources are usable, and unresolved missing data is reflected as blockers or assumptions.

agent_results.claim: Evidence dispatch boundary snapshot implementation covers git tracked/cached/other files via git ls-files, falls back to filesystem walk for non-git projects, and excludes the artifact root from mutation detection.

evidence.summary: gitFiles runs git ls-files -co --exclude-standard -z to capture tracked, cached, and other files, returning null if git fails.
evidence.why: This is the primary git-backed file enumeration that should cover all tracked and untracked files in git repositories.

evidence.summary: walkFiles recursively walks the project filesystem, excluding standard directories (.git, node_modules, dist, coverage, .next, .cache).
evidence.why: This is the fallback for non-git projects and supplements git ls-files to ensure complete coverage.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot combines git files and walk files (git ? [...git, ...walk] : walk), hashes each file with SHA-256, and excludes any file within allowedRoots (artifact directories).
evidence.why: This is the core boundary snapshot logic that creates before/after states for mutation detection.

evidence.summary: diffBoundarySnapshots compares before/after snapshots to detect added, modified, and deleted files, which become boundary violations.
evidence.why: This is how mutations are detected after agent execution completes.

evidence.summary: Runtime captures boundaryBefore snapshot, spawns agents, captures boundaryAfter snapshot, diffs them, and produces boundary violations in the result.
evidence.why: This is the execution flow that enforces the boundary constraint and blocks PRD readiness on violations.

evidence.summary: Test confirms boundary blocks project file mutation even when agent results pass, creating unexpected-project-write.txt and expecting blocked status with violated project_mutation.
evidence.why: This test verifies that any project file mutation triggers a boundary violation regardless of agent success.

evidence.summary: Git-backed boundary test creates an untracked file (untracked-mutation.txt) and expects blocked status with violated boundary and changes including the added file.
evidence.why: This verifies git ls-files -o captures untracked files and the boundary detects them as mutations.

evidence.summary: Gitignored boundary test creates .gitignore with .env, agent creates .env file, test expects blocked status and changes include added .env.
evidence.why: This confirms gitignored files outside excluded dirs are still detected as project mutations.

evidence.summary: Non-git boundary test modifies src/existing.txt and creates agent-scratch.txt in runtimeDir; expects blocked status with src/existing.txt in changes but agent-scratch.txt excluded.
evidence.why: This verifies fallback filesystem walk detects modifications and artifact root is properly excluded from boundary checks.

evidence.summary: excludedDir checks if directory is in [.git, node_modules, dist, coverage, .next, .cache], which walkFiles skips during recursive traversal.
evidence.why: This shows which directories are excluded from the fallback filesystem walk in non-git projects.

evidence.summary: Runtime creates artifact directory with mkdirSync(plan.output_dir, { recursive: true }) before boundaryBefore snapshot.
evidence.why: This confirms the artifact root exists before the baseline snapshot, so it is excluded from mutation detection.

evidence.summary: Execution policy declares allowed_write_roots as [repoRelative(outputDir, projectRoot)] and project_mutation as forbidden.
evidence.why: This documents the contract that only the artifact output directory is allowed for writes during evidence gathering.

assumptions: git ls-files -co --exclude-standard covers all relevant file categories (tracked, cached, other/untracked) for boundary snapshots
Combination of git ls-files and filesystem walk in git projects provides complete coverage without significant overlap performance cost
Test environment has git available for git-backed tests (tests return early if git init fails)
Artifact root exclusion works correctly because allowedRoots is passed before hashing files in buildBoundarySnapshot
risks: If git ls-files behavior changes across git versions, the boundary detection might miss or include unexpected files
The combination of git ls-files + walkFiles might double-count files or miss edge cases depending on git state
Non-git projects with very large file trees might hit performance or memory limits with recursive walkFiles
Tests are synthetic and may not cover real-world edge cases like submodules, worktrees, or unusual .gitignore configurations

result.notes: Implementation matches the demand: git-backed projects use git ls-files -co, non-git projects use filesystem walk, artifact root is excluded via allowedRoots, and boundary violations are detected via SHA-256 diffing. Test coverage confirms git untracked, gitignored, and non-git modification detection.

agent_results.claim: Explorer evidence verifies that boundary snapshots cover git tracked/cached/other files via git ls-files, include fallback walk for non-git projects, and exclude the artifact root from mutation detection, with test coverage confirming all three acceptance criteria.

evidence.summary: gitFiles runs git ls-files -co --exclude-standard -z, which covers tracked (default), cached (-c), and other/untracked (-o) files, returning null if git fails.
evidence.why: Verifies the primary claim that git-backed projects use git ls-files with the correct flags to capture all relevant file categories.

evidence.summary: walkFiles provides recursive filesystem walk fallback, excluding standard build/cache directories (.git, node_modules, dist, coverage, .next, .cache).
evidence.why: Verifies the fallback walk mechanism for non-git projects and as supplement in git projects.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot combines git and walk files with (gitFiles ? [...git, ...walk] : walkFiles), hashes each file with SHA-256, and excludes files within allowedRoots (artifact directories).
evidence.why: Verifies the core logic that combines both methods in git projects and uses walk only in non-git, while excluding allowed artifact roots.

evidence.summary: Git-backed boundary test creates untracked-mutation.txt and expects blocked status with boundary violation and changes including the added file.
evidence.why: Verifies that git ls-files -o captures untracked files and the boundary detects them as mutations.

evidence.summary: Gitignored boundary test creates .env file and expects blocked status with boundary violation, confirming gitignored files outside excluded dirs are detected.
evidence.why: Verifies that gitignored files are still detected as project mutations, confirming git ls-files coverage.

evidence.summary: Non-git boundary test modifies src/existing.txt and creates agent-scratch.txt in runtimeDir; expects blocked status with src/existing.txt in changes but agent-scratch.txt excluded.
evidence.why: Verifies fallback walk detects modifications in non-git projects and artifact root is properly excluded from boundary checks.

evidence.summary: Execution policy declares allowed_write_roots as [repoRelative(outputDir, projectRoot)] and project_mutation as forbidden.
evidence.why: Verifies the documented contract that only the artifact output directory is allowed for writes during evidence gathering.

assumptions: git ls-files behavior is stable across common git versions for the -co flags used
Combination of git ls-files and walkFiles in git projects does not introduce significant overlap or gap edge cases
Test environment properly simulates real-world git state variations
risks: git ls-files behavior might differ across git versions or in edge cases like submodules/worktrees
Large non-git projects might hit performance limits with recursive walkFiles

result.notes: Explorer evidence is comprehensive and directly supports the acceptance criteria. Code review confirms git ls-files -co covers tracked/cached/other files, walk provides fallback for non-git, and allowedRoots excludes the artifact root. Test coverage validates all three scenarios: git untracked files, gitignored files, and non-git project modifications with artifact root exclusion. Assumptions are reasonable and properly documented; no critical evidence is missing.
